title: People Pretending to Be Gay for Asylum
summary: The BBC found that some law firms and advisers are helping migrants pretend to be gay to stay in the UK. Migrants pay a lot of money for fake stories and evidence, like letters and photos, to claim asylum. They say they are gay and afraid to go back to countries like Pakistan or Bangladesh, where being gay is against the law. The UK offers protection to people in danger, but this system is being misused. The Home Office warns that anyone cheating will face legal trouble, including being sent away. An undercover investigation showed advisers helping students with expired visas make false claims at events like a support group meeting in London.

people who move to another country to live
to act like something is true when it is not

the UK government department that handles immigration and laws

Where was a support group meeting held in the investigation?

background_read: Asylum is when a country lets people stay because they are unsafe in their home country. In the UK, people can apply for asylum if they are in danger, like if they are gay in places where it is illegal. Some migrants come to the UK on visas for study or work, but when these expire, they might try to stay by making false claims. The BBC did a secret investigation to find out if advisers are helping them cheat the system.
Article_Structure: WHO: Law firms, advisers, migrants from Pakistan and Bangladesh, the BBC, and the Home Office
WHAT: Helping migrants pretend to be gay to get asylum with fake evidence
WHERE: UK, especially London, and countries like Pakistan and Bangladesh
WHY: To exploit the asylum system for money and to stay in the UK illegally

perspectives.description: The Home Office says cheating the system is wrong and will be punished.
The BBC found advisers are actively helping migrants make false claims.
